member.name: T:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.SwitchesToMigrate
member.summary: Only these switches will be migrated. This enum doesnt have any use, except to explicitly list the compiler options in AdditionalOptions project property that will be migrated

member.name: T:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.SwitchValueType
member.summary: These are types of values associated with the switches

member.name: F:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.SwitchValueType.SVT_Boolean
member.summary: Boolean value

member.name: F:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.SwitchValueType.SVT_String
member.summary: String value

member.name: F:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.SwitchValueType.SVT_MultiString
member.summary: This switch can occur multiple times and the final value is the ';' delimeted concat of all the individual occurences

member.name: T:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.CompSwitchInfo
member.summary: This class contains the migration info for a switch that we want to migrate

member.name: F:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.CompSwitchInfo.Switch
member.summary: This is the internal switch identifier Examples: 1. STM_SecureScoping

member.name: F:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.CompSwitchInfo.SwitchIDs
member.summary: This is the string passed to the compiler Examples: 1. /ss, /securescoping 2. @

member.name: F:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.CompSwitchInfo.SwitchValueType
member.summary: This is the type of the value associated with the switch Examples: 1. SVT_Boolean 2. SVT_MultiString

member.name: F:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.CompSwitchInfo.SwitchValue
member.summary: This is the final value of the switch 1. true 2. "path-a;path-b\\file-b"

member.name: F:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.CompSwitchInfo.SwitchProjectPropertyName
member.summary: This is the the name of property in the project file in which the value of this switch is stored

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.CompSwitchInfo.#ctor(Microsoft.Build.Conversion.SwitchesToMigrate,System.String[],Microsoft.Build.Conversion.SwitchValueType,System.Object,System.String)
member.summary: The constructor

member.name: T:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.AdditionalOptionsParser
member.summary:
Class: AdditionalOptionsParser Owner: ParthaD
This class contains the logic to parse the AdditionalOptions project property of v7.x J# projects and add the individual options as project properties of the upgraded projects.
AdditionalOptions project property in v7.x was basically a string that was passed ditto to the compiler. It was used to hold J# compiler options that didnt have an 1-1 equivalent project property. For v8.0 and beyond, each J# compiler option has a corresponding project property.
AdditionalOptions property string is broken down into list of options. White space (only ' ' and '\t') are considered as delimiters if not wrapped inside double quotes ("). NOTE: 1. Other unicode spaces or double quotes sequences not considered 2. Backslash (\) not considered as possible escape char for ".
Once broken down into individual options, only a few compiler options are seached for (viz. the options for which v8.0 has new project properties) Everything else is ignored.
Refer to SwitchesToMigrade enum for the switches that are migrated.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.AdditionalOptionsParser.ProcessAdditionalOptions(System.String,Microsoft.Build.Construction.ProjectPropertyGroupElement)
member.summary: One and only entry point to the functionality offered by this class

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.AdditionalOptionsParser.TokenizeAdditionalOptionsValue(System.String)
member.summary: This will tokenize the given string using ' ' and '\t' as delimiters The delimiters are escaped inside a pair of quotes If there is an unbalanced quote, EOL is treated as the closing quotes

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.AdditionalOptionsParser.ExtractSwitchInfo(Microsoft.Build.Conversion.CompSwitchInfo,System.String)
member.summary: If compSwitch is the compSwitchInfo compiler switch, then extract the switch args Return - true: if this is the switch (even if the switch args have error) - false: this is not the switch

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.AdditionalOptionsParser.PopulatePropertyGroup(Microsoft.Build.Construction.ProjectPropertyGroupElement)
member.summary: Populate the property group with the individual options

member.name: T:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.AssemblyResources
member.summary: This class provides access to the assembly's resources.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.AssemblyResources.GetString(System.String)
member.summary: Loads the specified resource string, either from the assembly's primary resources, or its shared resources.
member.returns: The resource string, or null if not found.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.LegacyFrameworkPaths
member.summary: Contains strings identifying hint paths that we should remove

member.name: T:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.VSProjectElements
member.summary: Contains the names of the known elements in the VS.NET project file.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.VSProjectAttributes
member.summary: Contains the names of the known elements in the VS.NET project file.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.XMakeProjectStrings
member.summary: Contains the names of some of the hard-coded strings we'll be inserting into the newly converted MSBuild project file.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.OldVSProjectFileReader
member.summary:
This class implements a custom text reader for the old VS7/Everett project file format. The old format allowed certain XML special characters to be present within an XML attribute value. For example,
<MyElement MyAttribute="My --> Value" />
However, the System.Xml classes are more strict, and do not allow the < or > characters to exist within an attribute value. But the conversion utility still needs to be able to convert all old project files. So the OldVSProjectFileReader class implements the TextReader interface, thereby effectively intercepting all of the calls which are used by the XmlTextReader to actually read the raw text out of the file. As we are reading the text out of the file, we replace all > (less-than) characters inside attribute values with ">", etc. The XmlTextReader has no idea that this is going on, but no longer complains about invalid characters.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.OldVSProjectFileReader.#ctor(System.String)
member.summary: Constructor, initialized using the filename of an existing old format project file on disk.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.OldVSProjectFileReader.Close
member.summary: Releases all locks and closes all handles on the underlying file.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.OldVSProjectFileReader.Peek
member.summary: Returns the next character in the file, without actually advancing the read pointer. Returns -1 if we're already at the end of the file.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.OldVSProjectFileReader.Read
member.summary: Returns the next character in the file, and advances the read pointer. Returns -1 if we're already at the end of the file.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.OldVSProjectFileReader.Read(System.Char[],System.Int32,System.Int32)
member.summary: Reads the specified number of characters into the caller's buffer, starting at the specified index into the caller's buffer. Returns the number of characters read, or 0 if we're already at the end of the file.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.OldVSProjectFileReader.ReadBlock(System.Char[],System.Int32,System.Int32)
member.summary: Not implemented. Our class only supports reading from a file, which can't change beneath the covers while we're reading from it. Therefore, a blocking read doesn't make sense for our scenario. (A blocking read is where you wait until the requested number of characters actually become available ... which is never going to happen if you've already reached the end of a file.)

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.OldVSProjectFileReader.ReadLine
member.summary: Reads a single line of text, and returns it as a string, not including the terminating line-ending characters. If we were at the end of the file, return null.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.OldVSProjectFileReader.ReadToEnd
member.summary: Reads the remainder of the file, and returns it as a string. Returns an empty string if we've already reached the end of the file.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.OldVSProjectFileReader.ReadLineIntoInternalBuffer
member.summary:
And this is where the real magic happens. If our currently cached "singleLine" has been used up, we read a new line of text from the underlying text file. But as we read the line of text from the file, we immediately replace all instances of special characters that occur within double-quotes with the corresponding XML-friendly equivalents. For example, if the underlying text file contained this:
<MyElement MyAttribute="My --> Value" />
then we would read it in and immediately convert it to this:
<MyElement MyAttribute="My --> Value" />
and we would store it this way in our "singleLine", so that the callers never know the difference.
This method returns true on success, and false if we were unable to read a new line (due to end of file).

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.OldVSProjectFileReader.ReplaceSpecialCharacters(System.String)
member.summary: This method uses a regular expression to search for the stuff in between double-quotes. We obviously don't want to touch the stuff OUTSIDE of double-quotes, because then we would be mucking with the real angle-brackets that delimit the XML element names, etc.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.OldVSProjectFileReader.ReplaceSpecialCharactersInXmlAttribute(System.Text.RegularExpressions.Match)
member.summary: This method is used as the delegate that is passed into Regex.Replace. It a regular expression to search for the stuff in between double-quotes. We obviously don't want to touch the stuff OUTSIDE of double-quotes, because then we would be mucking with the real angle-brackets that delimit the XML element names, etc.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.OldVSProjectFileReader.ReplaceSpecialCharactersInXmlAttributeString(System.String)
member.summary: This method actually does the replacement of special characters within the text of the XML attribute.
member.returns: New string with all the replacements (e.g. "&" becomes "&", etc.)

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.OldVSProjectFileReader.ReplaceNonEscapingAmpersands(System.String)
member.summary: This method scans the strings for "&" characters, and based on what follows the "&" character, it determines whether the "&" character needs to be replaced with "&". The old XML parser used in the VS.NET 2002/2003 project system was quite inconsistent in its treatment of escaped characters in XML, so here we're having to make up for those bugs. The new XML parser (System.Xml) is much more strict in enforcing proper XML syntax, and therefore doesn't tolerate "&" characters in the XML attribute value, unless the "&" is being used to escape some special character.
member.returns: New string with all the replacements (e.g. "&" becomes "&", etc.)

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.OldVSProjectFileReader.ReplaceAmpersandWithLiteral(System.String,System.Int32)
member.summary: Replaces a single instance of an "&" character in a string with "&" and returns the new string.
member.param: Original string where we should find an "&" character.
The index of the "&" which we want to replace.
member.returns: The new string with the "&" replaced with "&".

member.name: T:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter
member.summary: This class performs a project file format conversion from Visual Studio .NET 2002 or 2003 to MSBuild format (for Whidbey).

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.#ctor
member.summary: Default constructor. We need a constructor that takes zero parameters, because this class needs to be instantiated from COM.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.OldProjectFile
member.summary: The read/write accessor for the old project filename. This must be set by the consumer before calling Convert().

member.name: P:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.NewProjectFile
member.summary: The read/write accessor for the new project filename. This must be set by the consumer before calling Convert().

member.name: P:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.IsUserFile
member.summary: The read/write accessor for the boolean which tells the converter whether the project file we're converting is a "main" project file or a .USER file. Most of the conversion logic is identical for both types of files, but for example, one difference is that the "main" project file gets an <Import> tag inserted at the end.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.SolutionFile
member.summary: The read/write accessor for the solution file which contains this project being converted. This is used to look up information about the project-to-project references.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ConversionSkippedBecauseProjectAlreadyConverted
member.summary: Indicates if the last attempted conversion was skipped because the project is already in the latest format. This will always return false;

member.name: P:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ConversionWarnings
member.summary: This property returns the list of warnings that were generated during the conversion

member.name: P:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.IsMinorUpgrade
member.summary: Is the conversion a minor upgrade operation? Minor upgrade also means the converted project file can be opened in old VS as well, so we won't update the tools version.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.Convert
member.summary: This is the entry point method, which performs the project file format conversion. This method will overwrite "newProjectFile" if it already exists, so the caller of this method should confirm with the user that that's what he really wants to do.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.Convert(Microsoft.Build.BuildEngine.ProjectLoadSettings)
member.summary: This is the entry point method, which performs the project file format conversion. This method will overwrite "newProjectFile" if it already exists, so the caller of this method should confirm with the user that that's what he really wants to do.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.Convert(System.String)
member.summary: This is the entry point method, which performs the project file format conversion. This method will overwrite "newProjectFile" if it already exists, so the caller of this method should confirm with the user that that's what he really wants to do.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.DoConvert
member.summary: Helper method to convert given an engine

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.Initialize
member.summary: Initialize all member variables to get ready for a conversion.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ConvertInMemoryToMSBuildProject
member.summary: This is the entry point method, which performs the project file format conversion. This method will simply create a new XmlDocument in memory, instead of trying to write it to disk.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.DoRepairForAssetCompat
member.summary: returns 'false' if there was no repair required else does a repair and returns 'true'

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.RepairImportForAssetCompat(Microsoft.Build.Construction.ProjectImportElement)
member.summary: Repairs the given import element Change Import to use $(VSToolsPath), with Condition using $(VSToolsPath) e.g. From: Import Project="$(MSBuildExtensionsPath32)\Microsoft\VisualStudio\v10.0\WebApplications\Microsoft.WebApplication.targets" To: Import Project="$(MSBuildExtensionsPath32)\Microsoft\VisualStudio\v10.0\WebApplications\Microsoft.WebApplication.targets" Condition="false" Import Project="$(VSToolsPath)\WebApplications\Microsoft.WebApplication.targets" $(VSToolsPath) will be defined elsewhere in this upgrade to be: $(MSBuildExtensionsPath32)\Microsoft\VisualStudio\v$(VisualStudioVersion)

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ExtractImportTargetsString(System.String)
member.summary: Extracts the actual targets imported without the repair -pattern path e.g. from: $(MSBuildExtensionsPath32)\Microsoft\VisualStudio\v10.0\WebApplications\Microsoft.WebApplication.targets we will extract: WebApplications\Microsoft.WebApplication.targets

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.RequiresRepairForAssetCompat
member.summary: Checks if repair is required

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.HasRepairPattern(Microsoft.Build.Construction.ProjectImportElement)
member.summary: Check if the Import element has a repair pattern: $(MSBuildExtensionsPath)\Microsoft\VisualStudio\vX.X or $(MSBuildExtensionsPath)\Microsoft.VisualStudio.OfficeTools.targets

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.FixTargetFrameworkSubset
member.summary: Fixes properties in the project file. This was the Orcas SP1 way of handling framework profiles, and that way is now incompatible with the VS 2010 way of handling profiles.
member.returns: true if changes were required, false otherwise

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.FSharpSpecificConversions(System.Boolean)
member.summary: Performs conversions specific to F# projects (VS2008 CTP -> VS2012) and (VS2010 -> VS2012). This involves: changing the location of FSharp targets, and for 2008CTP, adding explicit mscorlib and FSharp.Core references.
member.param: if true, make the changes, otherwise, don't actually make any changes, but do report the return boolean as to whether you would make changes
member.returns: true if anything was (would be) changed, false otherwise

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.VBSpecificConversions
member.summary: Performs conversions specific to VB projects (VS2008 and VS2008 -> VS2010). This involves: Adding a set of nowarn settings to disable warnings added in VS2010 that break customers upgrading from previous releases.
member.returns: true if changes were required, false otherwise

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ConvertInMemory
member.summary: This is the entry point method, which performs the project file format conversion. This method will simply create a new MSBuild Project object in memory, instead of trying to write it to disk.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ConvertInMemory(Microsoft.Build.BuildEngine.Engine)
member.summary: This is the entry point method, which performs the project file format conversion. This method will simply create a new MSBuild Project object in memory, instead of trying to write it to disk.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ConvertInMemory(Microsoft.Build.BuildEngine.Engine,Microsoft.Build.BuildEngine.ProjectLoadSettings)
member.summary: This is the entry point method, which performs the project file format conversion. This method will simply create a new MSBuild Project object in memory, instead of trying to write it to disk.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.AddXMakePropertiesFromXMLAttributes(Microsoft.Build.Construction.ProjectPropertyGroupElement,System.Xml.XmlElement)
member.summary: Takes an XML element from an Everett project file, and loops through all its attributes. For each attribute, it adds a new XMake property to the destination project file in the property group passed in.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ProcessVisualStudioProjectElement(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation)
member.summary: Processes the <VisualStudioProject> XML element, and everything within it. As it is doing this, it will add stuff to the xmakeProject.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ProcessLanguageElement(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation)
member.summary: Processes the language (e.g. <CSHARP>) XML element, and everything within it. As it is doing this, it will add stuff to the xmakeProject.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.AddFinalPropertiesAndImports(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation,System.Boolean)
member.summary: Adds any last-minute additional properties such as FileUpgradeFlags and MyType, and also adds in the necessary Import tags.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ProcessBuildElement(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation)
member.summary: Processes the <Build> element, and everything within it. As it is doing this, it will add stuff to the xmakeProject.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ProcessSettingsElement(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation)
member.summary: Processes the <Settings> element, and everything within it. As it is doing this, it will add stuff to the xmakeProject.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ProcessConfigElement(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation)
member.summary: Processes the <Config> element, and everything within it. As it is doing this, it will add stuff to the xmakeProject, including new configuration-specific property groups.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ProcessPlatformElement(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation)
member.summary: Processes the <Platform> element, and everything within it. As it is doing this, it will add stuff to the xmakeProject, including new configuration-specific property groups.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ProcessInteropRegistrationElement(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation,Microsoft.Build.Construction.ProjectPropertyGroupElement)
member.summary: Processes the <InteropRegistration> element, and everything within it. As it is doing this, it will add extra properties to the configuration's property group.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ProcessReferencesElement(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation)
member.summary: Processes the <References> element, and everything within it. As it is doing this, it will add reference items to a new ProjectItemGroupElement.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ProcessReferenceElement(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation,Microsoft.Build.Construction.ProjectItemGroupElement)
member.summary: Processes the <Reference> element, and add an appropriate reference items to the referencesItemGroup.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ConvertClassicComReference(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation,Microsoft.Build.Construction.ProjectItemGroupElement,System.String)
member.summary: Given an element corresponding to a COM reference, create the appropriate element in the new project

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ConvertProjectToProjectReference(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation,Microsoft.Build.Construction.ProjectItemGroupElement,System.String,System.String@)
member.summary: Given an element corresponding to a P2P reference, create the appropriate element in the new project

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ConvertAssemblyReference(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation,Microsoft.Build.Construction.ProjectItemGroupElement,System.String)
member.summary: Given an element corresponding to a .NET Assembly reference, create the appropriate element in the new project

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.SearchForSolutionFile
member.summary: To convert project-to-project references correctly, we need some data out of the solution file. If we weren't given a solution file, then we search the project's directory and every parent directory all the way up to the root for the corresponding SLN file.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.RelativePathTo(System.String,System.String)
member.summary: Given a 'from' path and a 'to' path, compose a relative path from 'from' to 'to'.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.GetRelativePathToReferencedProject(System.String)
member.summary: Given a project guid for a referenced project, this method computes the relative path to that referenced project file from the current project file that is being converted. In order to do this, we need some information out of the SLN file.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ProcessImportsElement(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation)
member.summary: Processes the <Imports> element, and everything within it. As it is doing this, it will add "Import" items to a new ProjectItemGroupElement.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ProcessImportElement(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation,Microsoft.Build.Construction.ProjectItemGroupElement)
member.summary: Processes the <Import> element, and add an appropriate reference items to the importsItemGroup.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ProcessFilesElement(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation)
member.summary: Processes the <Files> element, and everything within it. As it is doing this, it will add the appropriate items to a new ProjectItemGroupElement.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ProcessIncludeElement(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation)
member.summary: Processes the <Include> element, and everything within it. As it is doing this, it will add the appropriate items to a new ProjectItemGroupElement.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ProcessFileElement(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation,Microsoft.Build.Construction.ProjectItemGroupElement)
member.summary: Processes the <File> element, and adds an appropriate item to the filesItemGroup.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.IsFilePresentButEmpty(System.String,System.String)
member.summary: Checks whether a file has content. If it is empty, returns true. If file does not exist we may be waiting for it to download asynchronously via source control so return false to leave it in the project

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ProcessFolderElement(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation,Microsoft.Build.Construction.ProjectItemGroupElement)
member.summary: Processes the <Folder> element, and adds an appropriate item to the filesItemGroup.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ProcessStartupServicesElement(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation)
member.summary: Processes the <StartupServices> element, and everything within it. As it is doing this, it will add new "StartupService" items to a new ProjectItemGroupElement.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ProcessServiceElement(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation,Microsoft.Build.Construction.ProjectItemGroupElement)
member.summary: Processes the <Service> element, and add an appropriate reference items to the startupServicesItemGroup.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ProcessOtherProjectSettingsElement(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation)
member.summary: Processes the <OtherProjectSettings> element, and everything within it. As it is doing this, it will add stuff to the globalPropertyGroup.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.ProcessUserPropertiesElement(Microsoft.Build.Construction.XmlElementWithLocation,System.Boolean@)
member.summary:
Processes the <UserProperties> element, and everything within it. Basically, this element and its contents end up going verbatim into the <ProjectExtensions> section of the newly converted MSBuild project file.
The one slight exception is that we do something special for Trinity conversion. Specifically, if we detect that this is a White Rabbit project, we add the flavor GUID for Triumph.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.FixCodeAnalysisPaths
member.summary:
Fix hard-coded fully qualified paths in Code Analysis properties.
Due to a bug in Whidbey configuration cloning, some Code Analysis properties in Whidbey project files contain fully qualified paths. They need to be detected and removed during project conversion so that Code Analysis will work on converted Whidbey projects.
member.returns: true if changes were required, false otherwise

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.FindPropertyIfPresent(Microsoft.Build.Construction.ProjectRootElement,System.String)
member.summary: Find the first property with the provided name in the ProjectRootElement. If none is found, returns null.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.GetProjectExtensionsString(System.String)
member.summary: Get the project extensions string with a particular ID, or empty string if it does not exist or there is no project extensions at all

member.name: M:Microsoft.Build.Conversion.ProjectFileConverter.SetProjectExtensionsString(System.String,System.String)
member.summary: Set a project extensions string with the provided Id, even if there is no project extensions tag at present
